16/10/25, 11:23 am - Aarya Tedla: #include <stdio.h>
#include <stdlib.h>

int h[100];

void heapify(int count);
void maxdel(int *count);
void adjust(int count);

int main() {
    int n, p;
    printf("Enter the number of elements: ");
    scanf("%d", &n);

    printf("Enter the elements: ");
    for(p = 0; p < n; p++) {
        scanf("%d", &h[p]);
    }

    heapify(n);

    printf("The heap is:\n");
    for(p = 0; p < n; p++)
        printf("%d\t", h[p]);

    // Example: Delete max
    maxdel(&n);

    printf("\nHeap after deleting max:\n");
    for(p = 0; p < n; p++)
        printf("%d\t", h[p]);

    return 0;
}

void heapify(int count) {
    int k, i, j, key;
    for(k = 1; k < count; k++) {
        i = k;
        j = (i - 1) / 2;
        key = h[i];
        while(i > 0 && h[j] < key) {
            h[i] = h[j];
            i = j;
            j = (i - 1) / 2;
        }
        h[i] = key;
    }
}

void maxdel(int *count) {
    if(*count <= 0) {
        printf("\nHeap is empty.\n");
        return;
    }
    int max = h[0];
    h[0] = h[*count - 1];
    (*count)--;
    adjust(*count);
    printf("\nThe max element in the heap is %d\n", max);
}

void adjust(int count) {
    int i, j, key;
    j = 0;
    i = 2 * j + 1;
    key = h[j];

    while(i < count) { // while left child exists
        if(i + 1 < count && h[i] < h[i + 1]) // choose larger child
            i++;
        if(key < h[i]) {
            h[j] = h[i];
            j = i;
            i = 2 * j + 1;
        } else {
            break;
        }
    }
    h[j] = key;
}

24/10/25, 8:35 am - Aarya Tedla: #include<stdio.h>
#include<stdlib.h>
#include<stdbool.h>

typedef struct thtree{
    int info;
    struct thtree *left;
    struct thtree *right;
    bool lthread;
    bool rthread;
}THTREE;

THTREE* insert(THTREE*,int);
void inorder(THTREE*);

int main()
{
    THTREE* root; 
    root=NULL;
    int choice;
    int x;
    while (1)
    {
        printf("Enter 1->Insertion \n 2->INORDER 3->EXIT \n");
        scanf("%d", &choice);
        switch (choice){
        case 1:
            printf("Enter value to be inserted");
            scanf("%d", &x);
            root=insert(root, x);
            break;
        case 2:
            inorder(root);
            break;
        default:
            exit(0);
        }
    }
    return 0;
}

THTREE* insert(THTREE *root,int x){
    THTREE* newnode;
    THTREE* temp;
    temp=root;
    newnode=(THTREE*)malloc(sizeof(THTREE));
    newnode->info=x;
    newnode->right=NULL;
    newnode->left=NULL;
    newnode->lthread=true;
    newnode->rthread=true
    if(root==NULL){
        root=newnode;
    }
    else{
        while(1){
            if(x<temp->info){
                if(temp->lthread==true){
                    newnode->left=temp->left; //predecessor
                    newnode->right=temp; //successor
                    temp->left=newnode;
                    temp->lthread=false;
                    break;
                }
                else{
                    temp=temp->left;
                }
            }
            else{
                if(temp->rthread==true){
                    newnode->left=temp; //predecessor
                    newnode->right=temp->right; //successor
                    temp->right=newnode;
                    temp->rthread=false;
                    break;
                }
                else{
                    temp=temp->right;
                }
            }
        }
    }
    return root;
}

void inorder(THTREE *root){
    if(root){
        inorder(root->left);
        printf("\t %d",root->info);
        inorder(root->right);
    }
}

25/10/25, 5:25 pm - Pavithraa: # app/services.py
from typing import Dict, Any
from app import model_loader

# ensure models are loaded (but don't re-load on every call if already loaded)
MODEL_SENTIMENT = model_loader.load_sentiment_model()
MODEL_COMPANY = model_loader.load_company_model()
MODEL_STOCK = model_loader.load_stock_model()

def get_sentiment_score(text: str) -> Dict[str, Any]:
    """Return label, score, numeric_score (positive/negative)."""
    pipeline = MODEL_SENTIMENT
    if pipeline is None:
        return {"label": "NEUTRAL", "score": 0.0, "numeric_score": 0.0}
    res = pipeline(text)
    if not res:
        return {"label": "NEUTRAL", "score": 0.0, "numeric_score": 0.0}
    label = res[0]["label"]
    score = float(res[0]["score"])
    numeric = score if label.upper().startswith("POS") else -score
    return {"label": label, "score": score, "numeric_score": numeric}

def get_company_connections(text: str) -> Dict[str,Any]:
    """Example wrapper for company connection model (returns mapped companies / scores)."""
    model = MODEL_COMPANY
    if model is None:
        # fallback stub
        return {"companies": [], "notes": "company model not available"}
    # Example: if company model expects text and returns dict
    try:
        return model.predict([text])[0]  # adapt to your model interface
    except Exception as e:
        print("company model predict error:", e)
        return {"companies": [], "error": str(e)}

def predict_stock_change(sentiment_score: float, company_features: Any) -> float:
    """Use stock model to predict percent-change. Adapt inputs to your saved model's expected features."""
    model = MODEL_STOCK
    if model is None:
        return 0.0
    # Example: you might create a feature vector combining sentiment and encoded company features
    # This is a placeholder; adapt to the features your stock_model expects.
    import numpy as np
    features = np.array([[sentiment_score, company_features.get("sector_code", 0) if isinstance(company_features, dict) else 0]])
    try:
        return float(model.predict(features)[0])
    except Exception as e:
        print("stock model predict error:", e)
        return 0.0

def full_predict(text: str) -> Dict[str, Any]:
    """High-level wrapper used by your endpoint: returns sentiment, companies, predicted_stock_change"""
    sent = get_sentiment_score(text)
    comps = get_company_connections(text)
    # convert company info into features your stock model expects
    # stub: example company_features dict
    company_features = {"sector_code": 1}  # replace with actual mapping from comps
    stock_change = predict_stock_change(sent.get("numeric_score", 0.0), company_features)
    return {
        "sentiment": sent,
        "companies": comps,
        "predicted_stock_change_percent": round(stock_change, 2)
    }

import os

# Define model paths
BASE_DIR = os.path.dirname(__file__)
MODEL_DIR = os.path.join(BASE_DIR, "..", "models")

IMPACT_MODEL_PATH = os.path.join(MODEL_DIR, "impact_regressor.pkl")

def load_impact_model():
    if os.path.exists(IMPACT_MODEL_PATH):
        print("✅ Impact model loaded successfully.")
        return joblib.load(IMPACT_MODEL_PATH)
    else:
        print("⚠️ Impact model not found.")
        return None

impact_model = load_impact_model()
25/10/25, 7:19 pm - Pavithraa: from app.services.model_loader import impact_model
import numpy as np

def prediction_service(db, data):
    """
    Uses trained impact model if available, else fallback to dummy logic.
    """
    event = data.get("event", "")
    company = data.get("company", "")
    sentiment = data.get("sentiment", 0.0)

    # Convert to numerical features
    features = np.array([[len(event), len(company), sentiment]])

    if impact_model:
        try:
            prediction = float(impact_model.predict(features)[0])
            return {"model_used": "impact_regressor.pkl", "predicted_impact": prediction}
        except Exception as e:
            return {"error": f"Model prediction failed: {str(e)}"}

    # fallback
    return {"result": "dummy prediction", "input": data}

04/11/25, 11:58 am - Aarya Tedla: #include <stdio.h>
#include <stdlib.h>

void creategraph(int);
void display(int);
void dfs(int, int);

int adj[10][10];
int visit[10];

int main() {
    int n, start;

    printf("Enter the number of vertices: ");
    scanf("%d", &n);

    creategraph(n);
    display(n);

    printf("Enter the starting vertex: ");
    scanf("%d", &start);

    printf("DFS traversal: ");
    dfs(start, n);

    return 0;
}

void creategraph(int n) {
    int i, j;
    printf("Enter the edges (source destination). Enter 0 0 to stop:\n");
    while (1) {
        scanf("%d%d", &i, &j);
        if (i == 0 && j == 0)
            break;
        else {
            adj[i][j] = 1;
            // adj[j][i] = 1;  // Uncomment this for undirected graph
        }
    }
}

void display(int n) {
    int p, q;
    printf("\nAdjacency Matrix:\n");
    for (p = 1; p <= n; p++) {
        for (q = 1; q <= n; q++)
            printf("\t%d", adj[p][q]);
        printf("\n");
    }
}

void dfs(int v, int n) {
    int w;
    printf("\t%d", v);
    visit[v] = 1;
    for (w = 1; w <= n; w++) {
        if (adj[v][w] == 1 && visit[w] == 0) {
            dfs(w, n);
        }
    }
}

18/11/25, 9:33 pm - Pavithraa: useEffect accepts two arguments. The second argument is optional.
useEffect(<function>, <dependency>)
If no dependencies are passed then rendering will happen continuously.
If an empty array is passed as dependency then it runs only on the first render.
If props or state values are given then render will be called and dependency values changes.

30/11/25, 1:14 pm - Aarya Tedla: I am uploading slide PDFs. Create MCQ-focused notes from them.

NOTE STYLE (Very Important)

Bullet points only

Short, crisp explanations (1–2 lines max)

Highlight keywords, definitions, rules, conditions, exceptions

Include memory triggers for tricky MCQs

No long theory

No fluff at all

CONTENT TO INCLUDE

Exact definitions (MCQ options rely on these)

Key properties / characteristics

Steps / rules / formulas

Differences (great for MCQs)

Tables for comparison

Edge cases & exceptions

Important diagrams only if essential (ASCII)

Typical MCQ traps (e.g., “Don’t confuse X with Y”)

CONTENT TO AVOID

Repetition from slides

STRUCTURE OF NOTES

Topic / Chapter Name

Quick Summary (3–5 bullets)

Must-Know Definitions

Concepts + Key points (very short explanations)

Formulas / steps

Comparison Table

Edge cases / exceptions / MCQ traps

Mini examples (optional, 1 line)

RULES

Fix formatting issues from slides

Merge duplicate points

Keep content strictly exam-oriented

Bold important terms

Ensure accuracy — MCQ exams punish small mistakes

OUTPUT

Clean headings

Bullet points

Medium length: not too detailed, not too shallow

10/12/25, 10:37 pm - Pavithraa: Sexual Side Effects

The most frequently reported adverse effects of Finasteride are sexual, such as low libido, erectile dysfunction and decreased semen volume.

These associated effects are thought to be caused by finasteride’s impact on some hormone levels. Although most of the users get these side effects only for a short period, few people experience long-lasting symptoms, which are sometimes still present after stopping the drug (Post-Finasteride Syndrome or PFS).

Gynecomastia Due to Hormonal Imbalance

Finasteride, by lowering DHT, can correct a hormone imbalance, but this correction may result in gynecomastia or breast tissue development in men. This happens when there are too many estrogens and not enough testosterone.

Gynecomastia can be uncomfortable physically and affect the psychological level of an individual, and in extreme cases, surgery is necessary.

Depression and Anxiety

For some users, the drug has been reported to have mood-related side effects on depression, anxiety, or worse, setting them up for suicidal thoughts. While not as expected, this pernicious effect is noteworthy and needs to be monitored closely.

Studies are being done in this area, but the change in hormonal balance may affect brain chemistry in vulnerable people.

Cognitive Side Effects

There are reports of memory problems and brain fog while on Finasteride, however. Based on rigorous clinical studies to date, these cognitive symptoms are poorly understood and may not have a causal link, and anecdotal reports suggest an association with Finasteride.

Allergic Reactions

Like all medications, there are also possible allergic reactions (e.g., a rash, itching, or swelling). Your immune system reacts severely, and you may be gasping for breath; this needs instant medical help.

Prostate Cancer Risk

Finasteride could be a potential contributing factor to high-grade prostate cancer. This is believed to be linked to Finasteride’s effect on DHT, which contributes to the development of prostate cells.

Finasteride is occasionally used to reduce the chance of benign prostate extension; however, men who have a record of prostate cancer need to communicate about this chance with their medical doctor.

Pregnancy-Related Risks

Pregnant women should never touch Finasteride because this drug can result in offspring birth defects and actually causes severe damage to male fetuses. This loss of pharmaceutical effect from crushed or broken tablets can allow active ingredients to be absorbed across the skin.

Maximizing Benefits, Minimizing Risks Of Minoxidil And Finasteride

While both also work very well for hair loss, the side effects associated with Minoxidil and Finasteride should be weighed in later. If you are looking for a way to balance the upside and downside, then here are some tips:

11/12/25, 10:01 pm - Pavithraa: #include<stdio.h>
#include<stdlib.h>
#include<stdbool.h>

typedef struct thtree{
    int info;
    struct thtree *left;
    struct thtree *right;
    bool lthread; 
} THTREE;

THTREE* insert(THTREE*, int);
void inorder(THTREE*);

int main()
{
    THTREE* root = NULL;
    int choice, x;
    while(1)
    {
        printf("Enter 1->Insertion \n2->INORDER \n3->EXIT\n");
        scanf("%d", &choice);
        switch(choice){
            case 1:
                printf("Enter value to be inserted: ");
                scanf("%d", &x);
                root = insert(root, x);
                break;
            case 2:
                inorder(root);
                printf("\n");
                break;
            default:
                exit(0);
        }
    }
    return 0;
}

THTREE* insert(THTREE *root, int x)
{
    THTREE* newnode = (THTREE*)malloc(sizeof(THTREE));
    newnode->info = x;
    newnode->lthread = true;
    newnode->left = NULL;
    newnode->right = NULL;

    if(root == NULL){
        root = newnode;
        return root;
    }

    THTREE* temp = root;
    THTREE* pred = NULL;

    while(temp != NULL){
        pred = temp;
        if(x < temp->info){
            if(temp->lthread == false)
                temp = temp->left;
            else
                break;
        }
        else{
            if(temp->right != NULL)
                temp = temp->right;
            else
                break;
        }
    }
    if(x < pred->info){
        newnode->left = pred->left; 
        newnode->right = pred;
        pred->lthread = false;
        pred->left = newnode;
    }
    else{
        newnode->left = NULL;
        newnode->right = pred->right;
        pred->right = newnode;
    }
    return root;
}

void inorder(THTREE *root)
{
    if(root == NULL)
        return;
    THTREE* curr = root;
    while(curr->lthread == false)
        curr = curr->left;

    while(curr != NULL){
        printf("%d ", curr->info);

        if(curr->lthread == true){
            curr = curr->right; 
        }
        else{
            curr = curr->right;
            while(curr != NULL && curr->lthread == false)
                curr = curr->left;
        }
    }
}

12/12/25, 6:50 am - Pavithraa: can u send rhe code for postfix eval
12/12/25, 6:51 am - Aarya Tedla: #include<stdio.h>
#include<stdlib.h>
#include<ctype.h>
#include<string.h>
#include<math.h>
char stack[100];
int top=-1;
int eval_stack[100];
int eval_top=-1;
int eval_pop()
{
    if(eval_top==-1)
    {
        printf("evaluation stack is empty\n");
        exit(0);
    }   
    else
    {
        int x=eval_stack[eval_top];
        eval_top--;
        return x;
    }
}
void eval_push(int x)
{
    if(eval_top==99)
    {
        printf("evaluation stack overflow\n");
        exit(0);
    }
    else
    {
        eval_top++;
        eval_stack[eval_top]=x;
    }
}
int isoperator(char ch)
{
    switch(ch)
    {
        case '+':
        case '-':
        case '*':
        case '/':
        case '^': return 1;
    }
    return 0;
}
int input_precedence(char ch)
{
    switch(ch)
    {
        case '+':
        case '-':return 1;
        case '*':
        case '/':return 3;
        case '^':return 6;
        case '(':return 9;
        case ')':return 0;
        case '#':return -1;
        default: return 8;
        
    }
}
int stack_precedence(char ch)
{
    switch(ch)
    {
        case '+':
        case '-':return 2;
        case '*':
        case '/':return 4;
        case '^':return 5;
        case '(':return 0;
        case ')':return 9;
        case '#':return -1;
        default: return 8;
        
    }
}
void push(char x)
{
    if(top==99)
    {
        printf("stack is full\n");
        exit(0);
    }
    else{
        stack[++top]=x;
    }
}
char pop()
{
    if(top==-1)
    {
        printf("stack is empty\n");
        exit(0);
    }
    else{
        char x=stack[top];
        top--;
        return x;
    }
}
char seek()
{
    if(top==-1)
    {
        printf("stack is empty\n");
        exit(0);
    }
    else
    {
        return stack[top];
    }
}
void convert_infix_to_postfix(char infix[],char postfix[])
{
    push('#');
    int i=0,j=0;
    while(infix[i]!='\0')
    {
        while(stack_precedence(seek())>input_precedence(infix[i]))
        {
            postfix[j++]=pop();
        }
        if(stack_precedence(seek())!=input_precedence(infix[i]))
        {
            push(infix[i]);
        }
        else{
            pop();
        }
        i++;
    }
    while(seek()!='#')
    {
        postfix[j++]=pop();
    }
    postfix[j]='\0';
}

int evaluate(char eval_postfix[])
{
    int i=0,x,y,z;
    char ch;
    while((ch=eval_postfix[i++])!='\0')
    {
        if(isalnum(ch))
        {
            eval_push(ch-'0');
        }
        else if(isoperator(ch))
        {
            x=eval_pop();
            y=eval_pop();
            switch(ch)
            {
                case '+':{ z=y+x; }break;
                case '-':{ z=y-x; }break;
                case '*':{ z=y*x; }break;
                case '/':
                        if (x == 0) {
                         printf("Error: Division by zero\n");
                        exit(1);
                        }
                        else{
                         z = y / x;
                        }
                         break;

                case '^': { z = (int)pow(y, x); } break;

            }
            eval_push(z);
        }
    }
    return eval_pop();
}

int main()
{
    char infix[100],postfix[100];
    char eval_postfix[100];
    int n,i=0,k=0,val;
    printf("\n enter the number of operands \n");
    scanf("%d",&n);
    printf("enter an infix expression \n");
    scanf("%s",infix);
    convert_infix_to_postfix(infix,postfix);
    printf("the postfix expression is: %s\n",postfix);
    while(postfix[i]!='\0')
    {
        if(isalpha(postfix[i]))
        {
            printf("enter the corresponding single digit number for  character %c\n",postfix[i]);
            scanf("%d",&val);
            eval_postfix[k++]=val+'0';  
        }
        else if(isoperator(postfix[i]))
        {
            eval_postfix[k++]=postfix[i];
        }
        else{
            printf("invalid character found \n");
            exit(0);
        }
        i++;
    }
    eval_postfix[k]='\0';  // Add null terminator
    int result=evaluate(eval_postfix);
    printf("the result is %d",result);
}

15/12/25, 2:55 pm - Pavithraa: ```const express = require('express');
const { MongoClient } =  require('mongodb');
const app = express();
const URI = "mongodb://localhost:27017/flights"
const client = new MongoClient(URI);

app.get('/flights', async (req, res) => {
    try {
        await client.connect();
        console.log("Connected to client\n");
        const db = client.db('flight_info');
        const collection = db.collection('flights');
        let query = await collection.find({}).toArray()
        res.send(query);
    }
    catch (err) {
        console.error(`Connection failed: ${err}`);
    }
    finally {
        await client.close();
    }
})

app.get('/flights/:from/:to', async (req, res) => {
    try {
        await client.connect();
        console.log("Connected to client\n");
        const db = client.db('flight_info');
        const collection = db.collection('flights');
        const from_fl = req.params.from;
        const to_fl = req.params.to;
        try {
            let query = await collection.find({ from: from_fl, to: to_fl }).toArray();
            res.send(query);
        }
        catch (err) {
            console.error(`An Error occurred: ${err}`);
            res.status(500);
        }
    }
    catch (err) {
        console.error(`Connection failed: ${err}`);
    }
    finally {
        await client.close();
    }
})


app.post('/flights', async (req, res) => {
    try {
        await client.connect();
        console.log("Connected to client\n");
        const db = client.db('flight_info');
        const collection = db.collection('flights');
        const { from_fl, to_fl, dept, arrv, flnum } = req.query;
        try {
            await collection.insertOne({ from: from_fl, to: to_fl, dept: dept, arrv: arrv, flnum: flnum });
            res.send('Update successful!');
        }
        catch (err) {
            console.log(`An error occurred: ${err}`);
            res.status(500);
        }
    }
    catch (err) {
        console.error(`Connection failed: ${err}`);
    }
    finally {
        await client.close();
    }
})


app.listen(8000, ()=>console.log('Server running on http://localhost:8000'));```

15/12/25, 5:44 pm - Pavithraa: <select id="videoSelect">
  <option value="video1.mp4">Video 1</option>
  <option value="video2.mp4">Video 2</option>
</select>

<button onclick="fetchVideo()">Fetch Video</button>

<div id="videoContainer"></div>

<script>
  function fetchVideo() {
    // Get the selected video URL
    let select = document.getElementById("videoSelect");
    let videoURL = select.value;

    // Get the placeholder div
    let videoDiv = document.getElementById("videoContainer");

    // Clear any existing video
    videoDiv.innerHTML = "";

    // Create a new video element
    let videoElem = document.createElement("video");
    videoElem.src = videoURL; // directly use the URL
    videoElem.controls = true; // show play/pause controls
    videoElem.width = 640;
    videoElem.height = 360;

    // Append the video to the div
    videoDiv.appendChild(videoElem);
  }
</script>

15/12/25, 9:52 pm - Aarya Tedla: Important webtech theory
Steps of End user OS request & respone
non-persistent http vs persistent http
http request line <method> <uri> <version> | http request methods get put post delete options head
http response line <version> <status code> <status message>
http status codes
https is encrypted version of http uses TLS(transport layer security)
form get method(appends form data to url , limit is 2048chars , visible in url)
form post method(appends data inside body , in the http request , large data , not visible in url)
difference between semantic html & non-semantic html
style element type attribute specifies MIME(multipurpose internet mail extension) type ex:text/css
string object table in slide 257 258 , all methods
theory related to document obj model , when webpage is loaded , browser creates DOM of page
the DOM represents and it can be manipulated , by scripting lang like javascript
A callback is a function passed as an argument to another function this allows a function to call another function
Synchronous calls vs asynchronous calls
what is a callback hell , when callsbacks are nested within other callbacks many levels deep,
async tasks are often dependant on the prev result
promise is javascript is used to handle async operations , better than deeply nested callbacks
promise has 3 states , pending , fulfilled , rejected.
XML means extensible markup language , represent data formats with hierarchical structure , structures , transfers, describes data
XML data format is three times as large as CSV
JSON means javascript object notation , represents data with use of commas and curly braces , smaller than XML
Difference between JSON & XML (around slide 47)
SPA means Single Page Application loads a single HTML page , then dynamically updates it with JavaScript as users interact
process & advantages of SPA & challenges bcoz of SPA
differences between SPA & MPA (around slide 65)
what is async await function , async function returns something wrapped in promise
AXIOS is js library to make http requests from browsers & node.js environments , GET , PUT , POST , DELETE & other http requests
other features of AXIOS : settings headers such as auth tokens content types. easily append query string to urls by providing
an object making get requests more dynamic , max time to wait for server response. Handling responses , global configuration
Single way data flow means react enforces unidirectional data flow from parents to child components (predictable datahandling , 
easier debugging , better maintainability)
VDOM means Virtual Document Object Model , light weight copy of actual DOM , react updates VDOM before syncing with real DOM.
slide 112 Reconciliation Process
JSX means Javascirpt XML , syntax used in react to simplify building interfaces (html like code in JS)
rules for HTML to JSX conversion 1. single root element 2. close all tags 3. use camelCase for Attributes
NPM means Node Package manager for NodeJS Modules , A package is NOdeJS is a reusable module
package.json file lists all dependancies the project needs (React , ReactDOM)
npm start(starts development server , auto-reload) , npm run build(creates /build folder)
npm test(runs test) , npm install (installs package)
components in react are reusable , independent code blocks, that define structure and behavious of UI (accept props & return elements)
features of components : only changed component re-renders , stateless by default , reusable piece of JS that output html elements
difference between stateless & stateful component (around slide 153)
styling in react refers to the various techniques used to apply visual design and presentation rules to individual components
map method allows u to transform each element of an array and return a new array with transformed values
The event object passed to React event handlers is a syntheticEvent
properties of a SyntheticEvent (bubbles, cancelable , currentTarget , EventPhase , type etc)
The form elements whose values are controlled by React State are called Controlled forms
difference between controlled forms and uncontrolled forms
what is useRef,useContext,useState,useReducer,useEffect , basic definitions and code
react router DOM is used for routing and navigation in React Applications , used for SPAs
features of NodeJS (Asynchronous , eventDriven , SingleThreaded , no buffering (NodeJS=Runtime + JS Library))
Nodejs theory (slide 83 to 110) Node.js vs Apache Differences wrt handling multiple requests
flow of common file request in PHP/ASP vs Node.js
what is event loop , event queue , libuv library?
Different between Multithreaded & Asynchronous Event Driven Model
Applications of node are (Data Intensive Real Time Application , SPA , JSON api based applications)
Node.js REPL(Read-Eval-Print-Loop) is like a playground for Node.js
http module methods (141 & 141 slides) & http request option (slide 146)
noedmon is a utility that will monitor for any changes and restart the server
Sync and asynchronous file operation methods difference
buffers in node.js are used to perform operations on raw binary data
streams are objects in node.js that let you read data from a source or write data to a destination in chunks instead
of loading everything at once
types of streams , readable , writable , dupex(TCP sockets) ,transform (modify data as it passes ,ex : compression)
stream is an eventemitter instance , throws several events at different instance of time (data , end , error , finish)
readable streams vs writable streams
A NoSQL database is a non-relational database that stores data in flexible, schema-less formats instead of tables.
RDBMS vs Mongo DB differences, mongoDB stores data in BSON(binary JSON)
service is an application that can be consumed by software and exposes its functionality through an API
API means application programming interface
WebServices : A software program that provides data and services to other applications using standard protocols(http/https)
characteristic of webservices (loose coupling , machine to machine interactions , interoperability ,platform independance)
REST APIs means Representational State Transfer , builds on HTTP protocol ,REST API theory (slide 50 to 70)
Steps for designing RESTful WebSerice : 
resources->URI struct->http methods->authentication&authorization->response format->http status codes->desc(slide 62-65)
Express.js is a backend web framework for node.js , runs on server side (fast , minimal , flexible)
advantages of express.js(built in routing , easy req body parsing , middleware , focus on application logic)
routing is determining how an application responds to a client request to a particular endpoint. 
which is uri and specific http req method
differences between url binding and url building
error handling how express catches & processor errors synchronously and asynchronously
next.js is a react framework , provides server side rendering(SSR) & static site generation (SSG) and api routes
SEO friendly , simplified routing ,performance, file based routing
SSG -> getStaticProps() , fetch data at build time , fast & seo friendly
SSR -> getServerSideProps() , fetch data per request on server , dynamic

27/01/26, 4:42 pm - Aarya Tedla: Dear Students,

This is to inform all 4th Semester CSE students that an Internal Hackathon will be conducted as part of CIE Ignite Season 02, which is integrated into the EIE II curriculum.

Hackathon Details

Date of Hackathon: 28th January 2025
Team Size: 6 members per team
Team Registration Deadline: 21st January 2025
Problem Statement

Teams may select a problem statement from the below problem statement bank https://docs.google.com/document/d/1PK1Rof2HP8CMvGj4xr3Lzhb4Ojk8AgMOUk3YvR4DJBc/edit?usp=sharing
OR
Propose their own problem statement
Presentation

Presentation template is CIE_Ignite-S02_Phase1_Template.pptx  .
Team Registration

All teams must submit their details using the following form on or before 22nd January 2025

 Team Registration Form:

https://forms.gle/Mh645mLcBi6fPUiB8


Academic Expectations

Students are expected to:

Clearly explain the problem context and the proposed solution
Demonstrate originality and creativity in their approach
Highlight the real-world relevance of the idea
Provide reasonable justification to indicate the practical feasibility of the solution
Present their ideas in a clear, structured, and professional manner
Importance

Participation in the hackathon is mandatory as part of EIE II.
Hackathon participation along with attendance will contribute 10% to the overall course evaluation.
Top teams will be selected for CIE mentorship in Phase 2 
In phase 3 Grand Finale: Top teams will pitch to an external jury, compete for cash prizes, and gain eligibility for CIE’s Pre-Incubation support, with potential continuation as Capstone projects.   
All students are expected to actively participate and ensure that team details are submitted within the stipulated deadline. Further details regarding the schedule will be shared in due course.

For any clarifications, please reply to this mail.

03/02/26, 7:26 pm - Aarya Tedla: PART B
1. Verifying Parent/Child Relationships (PID/PPID)
Answer:
PID (Process ID) uniquely identifies a process, while PPID (Parent Process ID) identifies the
process that created it. The parent/child relationship between the Hive and its larvae/husks can
be verified by checking that the PPID of each larva and husk equals the Hive’s PID using
commands like ps -o pid,ppid,stat,cmd --ppid <HIVE_PID> or by viewing the
hierarchy using pstree -p <HIVE_PID>.
Think about (one line):
PPID shows which process created the current process, and a process tree visually confirms
this hierarchy.
2. Parent Responsibility & Zombie Reaping
Answer:
Zombies exist until the parent reaps them because the kernel retains the child’s exit status in the
process table until the parent calls wait() or waitpid(). Reaping belongs to the parent so it
can collect this exit status and allow the kernel to remove the process entry; if the parent never
reaps, zombies accumulate and waste process table entries. Using WNOHANG makes
waitpid() non-blocking, allowing the parent to continue running while periodically checking
for exited children.
Think about (one line):
A zombie is already dead but unreaped, while a running process is still executing, and
non-blocking wait prevents the parent from freezing.
3. Zombie Creation
Answer:
The husk processes exit immediately after being created with fork(), but since the Hive parent
does not immediately call wait() or waitpid(), their exit status is not collected and they
become zombies. The zombies persist until the parent explicitly reaps them when the reap
command triggers waitpid().
Think about (one line):
Husks call exit() immediately, and zombies are reaped only when the parent explicitly
invokes wait()/waitpid().
4. Dormant Larvae
Answer:
A larva enters while (1) pause(); after malloc() fails so that it remains alive and visible
as a running process instead of terminating. If all larvae exited while the parent was sleeping,
they would become zombies or disappear, defeating the Hive’s goal of maintaining long-running,
resource-holding processes.
Think about (one line):
A paused process is alive and visible in ps, whereas an exited process is dead and may only
appear as a zombie

06/02/26, 2:16 pm - Aarya Tedla: Got it. Below is a **complete, start-to-finish, no-gaps, step-by-step guide for TWO Ubuntu laptops** to perform the **HTTP Persistent vs Non-Persistent Connections lab**.
You can follow this **blindly**, and it also matches **lab + viva expectations**.

I’ll assume:

* **Laptop 1 = Server (Ubuntu)**
* **Laptop 2 = Client (Ubuntu)**
* Both are on the **same Wi-Fi or LAN**

---

# 🔵 OVERALL IDEA (1-minute clarity)

* Server laptop runs **Apache HTTP Server**
* Client laptop runs **Firefox + Wireshark**
* Client loads a webpage with **10 large images**
* Wireshark captures traffic
* You compare:

  * Non-Persistent HTTP
  * Persistent HTTP with 2, 4, 6, 8, 10 connections

---

# PART 1: NETWORK SETUP (BOTH LAPTOPS)

## STEP 1: Connect both laptops to the SAME network

* Same Wi-Fi
  **OR**
* Same router via Ethernet

⚠️ This is mandatory.

---

## STEP 2: Check IP address on BOTH laptops

On **both laptops**, open Terminal:

```bash
ip a
```

Look for:

* Interface like `wlp…` (Wi-Fi) or `enp…` (Ethernet)
* IPv4 address like:

  ```
  192.168.x.x
  or
  10.x.x.x
  ```

### Example:

* Server IP: `192.168.1.10`
* Client IP: `192.168.1.20`

📌 **Note down the SERVER IP**

---

## STEP 3: Test connectivity (Client → Server)

On **Client laptop**:

```bash
ping <SERVER_IP>
```

Example:

```bash
ping 192.168.1.10
```

✔ Replies received → OK
❌ No reply → network problem (fix before continuing)

---

# PART 2: SERVER LAPTOP SETUP

## STEP 4: Install Apache HTTP Server (SERVER)

```bash
sudo apt update
sudo apt install apache2 -y
```

Check status:

```bash
sudo systemctl status apache2
```

You should see:

```
Active: active (running)
```

---

## STEP 5: Allow HTTP traffic (if firewall is enabled)

```bash
sudo ufw allow 80
sudo ufw reload
```

---

## STEP 6: Test Apache from CLIENT

On **Client browser**, open:

```
http://<SERVER_IP>
```

You should see **Apache default page**.

---

# PART 3: CREATE WEB CONTENT (SERVER)

## STEP 7: Generate 10 dummy images (RECOMMENDED METHOD)

Install ImageMagick:

```bash
sudo apt install imagemagick -y
```

Go to Apache folder:

```bash
cd /var/www/html
```

Remove old images (if any):

```bash
sudo rm -f img*.jpg
```

Generate 10 large images:

```bash
for i in {1..10}
do
  sudo convert -size 4000x4000 xc:lightgray \
  -gravity center -pointsize 200 -fill black \
  -annotate 0 "Dummy Image img$i" img$i.jpg
done
```

Verify size:

```bash
ls -lh img*.jpg
```

Each should be **≥ 2 MB**.

---

## STEP 8: Create HTML file

```bash
sudo nano /var/www/html/test.html
```

Paste:

```html
<html>
<body>
<h1>HTTP Persistent vs Non-Persistent Test</h1>

<img src="img1.jpg"><br>
<img src="img2.jpg"><br>
<img src="img3.jpg"><br>
<img src="img4.jpg"><br>
<img src="img5.jpg"><br>
<img src="img6.jpg"><br>
<img src="img7.jpg"><br>
<img src="img8.jpg"><br>
<img src="img9.jpg"><br>
<img src="img10.jpg"><br>

</body>
</html>
```

Save:

```
Ctrl + X → Y → Enter
```

---

## STEP 9: Fix permissions

```bash
sudo chmod 755 /var/www/html
sudo chmod 644 /var/www/html/*
```

---

## STEP 10: Final server test (CLIENT)

On **Client browser**:

```
http://<SERVER_IP>/test.html
```

✔ Page + all 10 images load → Server ready

---

# PART 4: CLIENT LAPTOP SETUP

## STEP 11: Install Firefox (if not present)

```bash
sudo apt install firefox -y
```

---

## STEP 12: Install Wireshark (CLIENT)

```bash
sudo apt install wireshark -y
```

When asked:

> Allow non-superusers to capture packets?

👉 **YES**

Add user to wireshark group:

```bash
sudo usermod -aG wireshark $USER
```

**Log out and log back in**

---

# PART 5: NON-PERSISTENT HTTP EXPERIMENT

## STEP 13: Disable KeepAlive (SERVER)

```bash
sudo nano /etc/apache2/apache2.conf
```

Find:

```
KeepAlive On
```

Change to:

```
KeepAlive Off
```

Restart Apache:

```bash
sudo systemctl restart apache2
```

---

## STEP 14: Configure Firefox (CLIENT)

In Firefox address bar:

```
about:config
```

Set:

```
network.http.keep-alive = false
network.http.max-persistent-connections-per-server = 0
```

---

## STEP 15: Clear browser cache (MANDATORY)

```
Ctrl + Shift + Delete → Clear All
```

---

## STEP 16: Capture traffic (CLIENT)

1. Open **Wireshark**
2. Select active interface (`wlp…` or `enp…`)
3. Start capture
4. Apply filter:

   ```
   http
   ```

---

## STEP 17: Load page (CLIENT)

Open:

```
http://<SERVER_IP>/test.html
```

Wait for all images to load.

Stop capture.

---

## STEP 18: Record timings

* Start time → first `GET /test.html`
* End time → last `img10.jpg`
* Calculate:

  ```
  Load Time = End − Start
  ```

Take **screenshot**.

---

# PART 6: PERSISTENT HTTP EXPERIMENTS

## STEP 19: Enable KeepAlive (SERVER)

```bash
sudo nano /etc/apache2/apache2.conf
```

Set:

```
KeepAlive On
```

Restart:

```bash
sudo systemctl restart apache2
```

---

## STEP 20: Persistent HTTP runs (CLIENT)

For each value **2, 4, 6, 8, 10**:

### Change Firefox setting:

```
network.http.keep-alive = true
network.http.max-persistent-connections-per-server = X
```

### For EACH run:

1. Clear browser cache
2. Start new Wireshark capture
3. Load `test.html`
4. Stop capture
5. Record times
6. Screenshot

---

# PART 7: RESULTS & SUBMISSION

## STEP 21: Observation Table

Create table with:

* Connection type
* No. of connections
* Start time
* End time
* Load time

---

## STEP 22: Graph Plot

* X-axis: `0, 2, 4, 6, 8, 10`
* Y-axis: Load time (seconds)
* Line graph

---

## STEP 23: Observation + Conclusion

Write short observation and conclusion (you already have these).

---

# FINAL CHECKLIST (DON’T MISS)

✔ Apache running
✔ Client can access server page
✔ Wireshark captures saved
✔ Cache cleared every run
✔ Screenshots attached
✔ Graph plotted

---

## 🧠 VIVA ONE-LINER

> “Persistent HTTP reduces page load time by reusing TCP connections, improving network efficiency compared to non-persistent HTTP.”

17/02/26, 11:01 pm - Pavithraa: ; software interrupt 0x201 to work with the 2 LEDs
MOV R1, #1000
LOOP1:
MOV R0, #1
SWI 0X201
SUBS R1, R1, #1
BNE LOOP1

MOV R2, #1000
LOOP2:
MOV R0, #2
SWI 0X201
SUBS R2, R2, #1
BNE LOOP2

MOV R3, #1000
LOOP:
MOV R0, #3
SWI 0X201
SUBS R3, R3, #1
BNE LOOP

; Register R0 should contain the value corresponding to which LED to light up - 1=right, 2=left, 3=both
17/02/26, 11:01 pm - Pavithraa: X: .WORD 100000

.EQU A, 0X80
.EQU B, 0X40
.EQU C, 0X20
.EQU D, 0X08
.EQU E, 0X04
.EQU F, 0X02
.EQU G, 0X01

TAB:
.WORD A|B|C|D|E|G;0
.WORD B|C;1
.WORD A|B|F|E|D;2
.WORD A|B|F|C|D;3
.WORD G|F|B|C;4
.WORD A|G|F|C|D;5
.WORD A|G|E|D|C|F;6
.WORD A|B|C;7
.WORD A|B|C|D|E|F|G;8
.WORD A|B|F|C|D|G;9
.WORD E|G|A|B|C;A
.WORD G|E|F|C|D;B
.WORD A|G|E|D;C
.WORD A|B|C|D|E|G;D
.WORD A|G|F|E|D;E
.WORD A|G|F|E;F

LDR R5, =TAB
MOV R2, #16

UPCOUNT:
LDR R0, [R5], #4
SWI 0X200
BL DELAY
SUBS R2, R2, #1    
BNE UPCOUNT        
B EXIT

DELAY:
LDR R6, =X
LDR R7, [R6]

LOOP:
SUBS R7, R7, #1   
BNE LOOP
MOV PC, LR

EXIT:
SWI 0X11
17/02/26, 11:01 pm - Pavithraa: ;Down Counter
X: .WORD 1000000

.EQU A, 0X80
.EQU B, 0X40
.EQU C, 0X20
.EQU D, 0X08
.EQU E, 0X04
.EQU F, 0X02
.EQU G, 0X01

TAB:
.WORD A|G|F|E
.WORD A|G|F|E|D
.WORD B|C|D|E|F
.WORD A|G|E|D
.WORD G|E|F|C|D
.WORD E|G|A|B|C|F
.WORD A|B|F|C|D|G
.WORD A|B|C|D|E|F|G
.WORD A|B|C
.WORD A|G|E|D|C|F
.WORD A|G|F|C|D
.WORD G|F|B|C
.WORD A|B|F|C|D
.WORD A|B|F|E|D
.WORD B|C
.WORD A|B|C|D|E|G

LDR R5, =TAB
MOV R2, #16

DOWNCOUNT:
LDR R0, [R5], #4
SWI 0X200
BL DELAY
SUBS R2, R2, #1    
BNE DOWNCOUNT        
B EXIT

DELAY:
LDR R6, =X
LDR R7, [R6]

LOOP:
SUBS R7, R7, #1   
BNE LOOP
MOV PC, LR

EXIT:
SWI 0X11
17/02/26, 11:01 pm - Pavithraa: ;Both
X: .WORD 1000000

.EQU A, 0X80
.EQU B, 0X40
.EQU C, 0X20
.EQU D, 0X08
.EQU E, 0X04
.EQU F, 0X02
.EQU G, 0X01

TAB:
.WORD A|B|C|D|E|G
.WORD B|C
.WORD A|B|F|E|D
.WORD A|B|F|C|D
.WORD G|F|B|C
.WORD A|G|F|C|D
.WORD A|G|E|D|C|F
.WORD A|B|C
.WORD A|B|C|D|E|F|G
.WORD A|B|F|C|D|G
.WORD E|G|A|B|C|F
.WORD G|E|F|C|D
.WORD A|G|E|D
.WORD B|C|D|E|F
.WORD A|G|F|E|D
.WORD A|G|F|E

START:
LDR R5, =TAB
MOV R2, #16

UP:
LDR R0, [R5], #4
SWI 0X200
BL DELAY
SUBS R2, R2, #1
BNE UP

LDR R5, =TAB
ADD R5, R5, #60
MOV R2, #16

DOWN:
LDR R0, [R5], #-4
SWI 0X200
BL DELAY
SUBS R2, R2, #1
BNE DOWN

B START

DELAY:
LDR R6, =X
LDR R7, [R6]
LOOP:
SUBS R7, R7, #1
BNE LOOP
MOV PC, LR

EXIT:
SWI 0X11

18/02/26, 8:23 pm - Pavithraa: 1. Which risk refers to problems in building the technology itself?
Technology risk

2. Which of the following belongs to the Pretotyping Manifesto?
Data beats opinion

3. Which action best matches the idea of pretotyping first?
Running a small fake-door test with users

4. The key difference between a prototype and a pretotype is that a prototype mainly checks:
Can we build it?

5. The “Law of Market Failure” states that most new products fail:
Even if competently executed

6. Market risk is primarily about:
Whether customers want the offering

7. In FLOP, which factor relates to sales and distribution not reaching users?
Launch

8. When users try a product but “simply don’t care,” it is mainly a failure of:
Design

9. Person A builds a simple landing page for an app idea and checks how many users click “Sign Up” before building the actual app. Person B spends two weeks building a working demo with basic features. What are A and B doing respectively?
A – Pretotyping, B – Prototyping

10. Why is it important to ensure you are building "The Right It" before a full launch?
Because most new products fail even if they are competently executed.

11. Which of the following scenarios best represents product risk?
The product failing to meet reliability and performance expectations

12. Which activity focuses on answering: “Would users actually use this?”
Pretotyping

13. The FLOP framework is used to distinguish between a flawed business model and poor execution. In this context, FLOP stands for:
Failure of Launch, Operations, and Premise

14. Spending weeks building a prototype before checking user interest mainly increases:
Premise risk

15. Pretotyping primarily helps reduce:
Market risk

02/03/26, 7:59 pm - Aarya Tedla: .DATA
A:      .WORD   0

.TEXT

MAIN:
        LDR     R4, =A      ; R4 = address of A
        MOV     R1, #11
        MOV     R2, #10
        MOV     R3, #2
        STMFD   R13!, {R1,R2,R3}   ; push parameters
        BL      ADDFun
        STR     R0, [R4]
        SWI     0x11

; ---------------- ADDFun ----------------

ADDFun:
        LDMFD   R13!, {R4,R5,R6}   ; pop 3 params
        ADD     R0, R4, R5         ; R0 = R4 + R5
        STMFD   R13!, {R0,R6,LR}   ; push sum, multiplier, LR
        BL      MULFun
        LDMFD   R13!, {LR}         ; restore LR
        MOV     PC, LR


; ---------------- MULFun ----------------

MULFun:
        LDMFD   R13!, {R4,R5}      ; pop sum and multiplier

        MUL     R0, R4, R5

        MOV     PC, LR
